coredata.dc:title: Promoting effect of cellulose-based carbon dots at different concentrations on multifunctional photocatalytic degradation of dyes by ZnO
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shaopeng
Chen, Xueqi
Cheng, Zhuoying
Luo, Suyue
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Guo, Minghui
Gao, Xing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel cellulose based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) doped ZnO composite, ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40), was developed as a photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of four dyes. X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), and transient photocurrent measurements were carried out to study the effects of different concentrations of CDs on the morphology and photocatalytic properties of ZnO/CDs−x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) composites. When the concentration of CDs increased, the structure of ZnO/CDs−x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) changed from a peony like structure (x = 10, 20) to a hexagonal prism like structure (x = 30, 40) and assembled into a flower like structure. Flower like ZnO/CDs-20 showed the best photocatalytic performance and good durability under UV light, and three types of excitation effects were observed: mixed light > sunlight > visible light. It was found that the addition of CDs with a concentration of 20 mL in ZnO can significantly enhance the photoabsorption and the separation of photogenerated carriers, which is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
CDs
Photocatalysis
Nanocomposite
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD-zinc oxide composite material

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31971587 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs, (b) CDs in UV and visible light, (c) a histogram of the particle size distribution of CDs.

Preparation process and formation mechanism of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40): (1) Cellulose is carbonized to form CDs. (2) Adsorption and bonding of Zn2+ on CDs. (3) Nucleation and growth of ZnO microcrystals leads to the formation of ZnO/CDs-x.
(a) XRD patterns of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40); (b–d) XPS spectra of ZnO/CDs-20.
SEM images of (a) pure ZnO, (b) ZnO/CDs-10, (c) ZnO/CDs-20, (d) ZnO/CDs-30, (e) ZnO/CDs-40 nanocomposite.
TEM images of (a) pure ZnO, (b) ZnO/CDs-20, (c) ZnO/CDs-40. (The inset of b and c shows the high-resolution TEM images).
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectra of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40). (b) Image of band gap of ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40).

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB by different products. (b) Photocatalytic degradation of different organic dyes by ZnO/CDs-20 (The illustration in a and b shows a physical picture of photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by ZnO/CDs-20).

(a) Kinetic reaction equation curves of ZnO and CDs/ZnO over MB, (b) reaction rates of photocatalytic degradation of MB over a photocatalyst-free solution (blank), pure ZnO, ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40).
Cycle number of photocatalytic degradation of MB by ZnO/CDs-20.
Photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light (a), sunlight (b).
Schematic diagram of photoexcited electron (a:ultraviolet, b:visible light, c:sunlight).
Transient photocurrent response of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites.
Quenching experimental curve of ZnO/CD-20 photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue.
Mechanism of photosensitized degradation of dye over ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposite under light.
Experimental conditions of solar photocatalytic organic dyes.

head.ce:title: Promoting effect of cellulose-based carbon dots at different concentrations on multifunctional photocatalytic degradation of dyes by ZnO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sports and Human Sciences, Post-doctoral Mobile Research Station of Harbin Sport University, Harbin Sport University, Harbin, 150008, PR China

Post-doctoral Mobile Research Station of Harbin Sport University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel cellulose based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) doped ZnO composite, ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40), was developed as a photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of four dyes. X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), and transient photocurrent measurements were carried out to study the effects of different concentrations of CDs on the morphology and photocatalytic properties of ZnO/CDs−x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) composites. When the concentration of CDs increased, the structure of ZnO/CDs−x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) changed from a peony like structure (x = 10, 20) to a hexagonal prism like structure (x = 30, 40) and assembled into a flower like structure. Flower like ZnO/CDs-20 showed the best photocatalytic performance and good durability under UV light, and three types of excitation effects were observed: mixed light > sunlight > visible light. It was found that the addition of CDs with a concentration of 20 mL in ZnO can significantly enhance the photoabsorption and the separation of photogenerated carriers, which is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of human society and the continuous growth of industry, nonrenewable resources are rapidly consumed, and industrial pollution has become increasingly severe [1]. As a very important natural resource, water pollution is a very difficult problem. Owing to the increasing demand for textiles, paper, and plastics in recent years, organic dyes and their wastewater have become one of the main sources of water pollution. Organic dyes have high stability, and they can easily remain in water. Among the numerous water pollution treatment technologies, energy-saving, green, and efficient photocatalytic technologies are the best [1–3]. The key to photocatalysis technology is the catalyst. Many types of photocatalysts are used in water treatment [4–9], among which semiconductors are the main type of catalysts. Semiconductors are mainly used to mineralize pollutants to CO2, water, and inorganic acids [2]. The most commonly used semiconductors are titanium dioxide (TiO2) and zinc oxide (ZnO) [10]. TiO2 is particularly well known for its use in photocatalytic water purification, but it requires longer recovery time between photocatalytic cycles. ZnO has unique physical, chemical, and biocompatibility characteristics, so it is often used in the photocatalytic degradation of dyes. ZnO has a wide band gap similar to TiO2, and it has unique chemical defects and electron mobility, even higher than that of TiO2. However, the recombination speed of photogenerated electron hole pairs in single-phase semiconductors is fast, and it has poor light absorption, leading to low photocatalytic efficiency [2,11–13].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with diameters of less than 10 nm. They have excellent fluorescence properties, low cost, nontoxicity, good biocompatibility, and photoinduced electron transfer properties [14,15]. As a nanomaterial, CDs are only a few nanometers in diameter. Moreover, abundant raw materials are available for the preparation of CDs, such as wood flour, wood processing residues, peels, and various residues [16–21]. CDs can be prepared in many ways. From the perspective of a carbon source, the preparation methods of CDs can be divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up.” The “top-down” methods include arc discharge [22], laser ablation [23,24], and electrochemical synthesis [25]. “Bottom up” methods include chemical oxidation [26], combustion [27], hydrothermal/solvothermal [28,29], and microwave synthesis [17]. Among them, hydrothermal method is more commonly used, because it is efficient, simple, safe, and applicable for a wide range of raw materials [30]. Since the discovery of CDs by Xun in 2004 [31], CDs have been used in many fields, such as biomedicine, anticounterfeiting, sensing, catalysis, and photovoltaic devices [32–36].

Although CDs have great application prospects in the field of photocatalysis, there are still many problems and challenges worthy of further research. At present, there are few literature reports on the effects of doping concentration of carbon dots on the physical and chemical properties and photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts. Therefore, it is necessary to study the effects of carbon dots with different doping concentrations on the properties of carbon dot based composites and the modification of their photocatalytic performance, in order to determine the optimal doping concentration of carbon dots in photocatalysts. In this work, cellulose-based fluorescent CDs and novel ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) composites were prepared using hydrothermal method with nanocellulose as the carbon source. The effects of doping of CDs on the phase structure, morphology, chemical composition, pore structure, optical absorption, and charge separation of the samples were analyzed by conducting various scientific experiments. The optimal doping amount of CDs (x = 20) in the composite was determined by the photocatalytic degradation experiment of methylene blue (MB) under UV light. Then, the photocatalytic performance, stability, and photocatalytic mechanism of ZnO/CDs-20 for degradation of MB, Eosin Y, Congo red, and Rhodamine B dyes were investigated. Moreover, the photocatalytic performance of ZnO/CDs-20 for MB degradation under different light sources was studied.

Nanocellulose was obtained from the North Century Cellulose Material Co., Ltd. Citric acid, zinc acetate dihydrate, sodium hydroxide, and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). They were of laboratory grade and used without further purification.

CDs were prepared following a literature method [37]. First, 10 g of 1% nanocellulose uniformly dispersed in 40 g of water was weighed out, and then 100 μL of ethylenediamine and 0.1 g of citric acid were added. The mixture was stirred evenly, and transferred to a 50 mL sealed Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, it was heated in a controlled oven at the required temperature of 200 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the resulting CD solution was suction filtered and placed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da for dialysis for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD-zinc oxide composite material
ce:section.ce:para: According to the method described in the literature [2], different volumes of CD solutions (10 mL, 20 mL, 30 mL, 40 mL) were taken and diluted to 40 mL with water to prepare CD solutions with different concentrations. Then, 0.2195 g (1 mmol) of zinc acetate dihydrate, and 0.4 g (10 mmol) of sodium hydroxide were weighed and sequentially added to the CD solution prepared above. The mixture was stirred until the solid was completely dissolved. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and sealed, and kept at 90 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the obtained white solid was dried at 60 °C for 6 h and annealed at 400 °C for 2 h. The samples were labeled as pure ZnO, ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) according to the different ratios of CDs.

The products were characterized using a Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI, Holland) operated at 5.0 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out using a D/max-2200VPC X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV and 30 mA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using a THERMO spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA), with Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). The optical absorption features and UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2550).

The photocatalyst sample was irradiated with a 300 W mercury lamp simulating the sunlight. The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by the photodegradation of MB in water. In each experiment, 30 mg of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) was suspended in 30 mL of 20 mg/L MB aqueous solution. Before irradiation, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 2 h to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between photocatalyst and MB. Then, the solution was exposed to a light source for irradiation under magnetic stirring. Within the given time interval, 2.5 mL aliquots were extracted for MB concentration analysis. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2550) was used to monitor photocatalytic degradation. In addition, to verify the catalytic performance of the catalyst for other organic dyes, Congo red (CR), Eosin Y (EY), and Rhodamine B (RB) were selected for comparative experiments with ZnO/CDs-20 catalyst and monitored by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 a shows that the size of CDs with a circular shape is less than 10 nm. The high-resolution image at the top right of Fig. 1a shows that the lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction facet of sp2 graphitic carbon. This indicates that there is at least one ordered part in CDs [38]. Fig. 1b shows the luminescence of CDs under UV and visible-light irradiation. The CDs emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation. Fig. 1c shows the particle size distribution histogram of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the diameter of CDs is between 5 and 10 nm, and the average particle size is 7.3 nm, which can help ensure good fluorescence performance of CDs [39,40].

In this study, ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) samples were prepared using a two-step hydrothermal method. Fig. 2 shows a brief synthesis route of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40). First, cellulose was carbonized to form CDs which contained many oxygen-containing groups on their surface [37]. After the addition of (CH3COO)2Zn, the Zn2+ ions in the aqueous solution reacted with hydroxyl groups and were fixed around the CDs. After adding NaOH, Zn2+ reacted with excess NaOH to generate
Z n
(
O H
)
4
2 −
and ZnO2−. After hydrothermal treatment at 90 °C, a large amount of ZnO was formed, and finally the ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) samples were obtained.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40). The results show that all the diffraction peaks of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) are consistent with those of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (JCPDS No. 36-1451). In addition, due to the low content of CDs, the diffraction peak of CDs is not detected in the composite. Further observation shows that the diffraction peak of ZnO/CDs-x becomes sharper, and the peak intensity increases with the increase in carbon content. This shows that the crystallinity of ZnO improves with the increase in the content of CDs [41].

The X-ray photoelectron spectra of ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3b–d, and the elemental composition of ZnO/CDs-20 can be obtained. The existence of Zn, C, and O is observed in the survey spectrum (Fig. 3b). The higher-resolution spectrum of Zn 2p3/2 in Fig. 3c shows that the level of Zn 2p3/2 is 1021.13 eV. The C1s XPS spectrum of ZnO/CDs-20 is shown in Fig. 3d, which clearly shows three main components corresponding to carbon atoms in different functional groups: nonoxidized C–C bond (284.82 eV), epoxy and hydroxyl C–O (285.58 eV), and carbonyl CO (288.83 eV). The peak at 291.63 eV can be attributed to the π–π※ satellite of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy modified aromatic conjugated units in CDs [42]. Moreover, the peak at 290.63 eV indicates a partially modified π structure in ZnO/CDs-20.

SEM images in Fig. 4 a–e shows the morphologies and microstructures of pure ZnO nanoparticles, and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40). As seen from Fig. 4a, the morphology of pure ZnO consists of a large number of irregular flakes agglomerated with a disordered distribution. Fig. 4b and c shows the morphology of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20) samples with a small amount of CDs added. The morphology of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20) is still sheet like. Unlike pure ZnO, the nanosheets in ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20) are aggregated into microspheres with a diameter of about 1 μm. When the amount of CD solution reaches 20 mL, the composite shows a peony like structure. When more CDs are added, the microstructure of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 30, 40) changes from a sheet to a hexagonal prism structure (Fig. 4d and e).

The TEM and high-resolution TEM images of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) are shown in Fig. 5 . The TEM image of pure ZnO in Fig. 5a shows disordered flakes with some aggregation. Fig. 5b shows the TEM image of ZnO/CDs-20, which presents a flower-like structure, and the middle part cannot show a clear structure due to its large size. Fig. 5c shows that the flower-like structure is composed of irregular nanorods with a size of 3–4 μm, consistent with SEM observations. The lattice structure of ZnO is shown in the high-resolution image in the upper right corner of Fig. 5b and c. The lattice stripe spacings are 0.28 nm and 0.52 nm, respectively, corresponding to the {0001} and {0110} planes of hexagonal (wurtzite) zinc oxide, respectively [2].

Fig. 6 a shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) composites. Compared with pure ZnO, the absorption edge of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) is red shifted, and the absorbance is increased. This can be attributed to the introduction of CDs. The electrons in CDs absorb photons, transition to the excited state, and then release energy back to the ground state, thus providing enhanced optical absorption. The band gap energy (Eg) of the prepared samples was plotted and calculated using the following formula:
(αhν)n = A(hν − Eg)
where A is the proportional constant; α represents the direct and indirect band gap transitions; “n” is 2 and 0.5. The Eg values of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) are calculated to be 3.23 eV, 3.20 eV, 3.13 eV, 3.18 eV, and 3.21 eV, respectively (Fig. 6b). The results show that the addition of CDs reduces the band gap of ZnO, and ZnO/CDs-20 has the lowest band gap energy, which is probably one of the reasons for its better photocatalytic performance than other samples.

Fig. 7 a shows the results of photocatalytic degradation of organic dye MB by different products, where C0 is the initial concentration after adsorption equilibrium and Ct is the residual concentration of MB at t time. MB shows different degrees of degradation in simulated sunlight without a photocatalyst. Compared with pure ZnO (degradation rate is 72.04% in 35 min), the catalytic degradation efficiency of ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) nanocomposites with CDs is significantly improved, and the degradation efficiency of MB is more than 90% in 35 min. The degradation effect of ZnO/CDs-20 is the best, and MB is almost completely degraded in 30 min. Fig. 7b shows the degradation of four different organic dyes (MB, EY, CR, and RhB) by ZnO/CDs-20. The best degradation effect of ZnO/CDs-20 photocatalyst is observed for CR, and the conversion rate is almost 100% in about 20 min. The degradation of RhB is the worst, only 70.49% in 35 min.

The linear simulation curves of photodegradation kinetics of MB dye in different samples are shown in Fig. 8 a. When the irradiation time is taken as the abscissa and ln (C0/Ct) is taken as the ordinate, the fitting curve is a straight line. The calculated K values are 0.0069, 0.0361, 0.1209, 0.1612, 0.0926, and 0.1019 (Fig. 8b). The K value of ZnO/CDs-20 is the largest, followed by ZnO/CDs-10 and ZnO/CDs-40, and that of ZnO/CDs-30 is the smallest. K values of all the composites are higher than that of pure ZnO, consistent with the photocatalytic performance of the samples.

Reusability is an important index of photocatalyst application. To test the photocatalytic stability of ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites, a photocatalytic cycle experiment was carried out, as shown in Fig. 9 . Four cycle experiments were performed. MB was almost completely degraded in the first and second cycle experiments, while it was not completely decomposed in the third and fourth cycle experiments. However, the trend shows that MB is completely decomposed with the increase in time. This shows that ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites have good photocatalytic stability, which is also conducive to their application in the photocatalysis field.

Fig. 10 a shows the photocatalytic effect of ZnO/CDs-20 catalyst on degradation of 20 mg/L MB under visible light. The degradation results show that ZnO/CDs-20 still had a good photocatalytic effect under visible light, and MB was almost completely degraded in 120 min. Compared with the experimental results of simulated sunlight, the photocatalytic efficiency under visible light is lower, and the catalytic time is longer, probably because of different wavelengths of light.

The results of photocatalytic degradation of MB by ZnO/CDs-20 under sunlight are shown in Fig. 10b. Table 1 shows the general conditions of this experiment, including temperature, time, and place; the data were taken from Huawei Weather. About 2.5 mL of solution was extracted at intervals for UV detection. The concentration of dye can be obtained from its UV–Vis spectrum, and the catalytic performance results can be obtained by calculation. The catalyst can also catalyze the degradation of organic dye MB under the irradiation of sunlight, and the catalytic effect is better than that under visible light, but slightly worse than that under a combined irradiation of UV and visible light. After 45 min of direct sunlight, organic dyes can be completely degraded. Although the degradation of organic dyes by sunlight is affected by natural conditions, such as weather and geographical location, the degradation by sunlight can reduce the consumption of resources, increase the utilization of solar energy resources, and reduce the cost.

During photocatalysis, light is an indispensable and important condition. The main factors affecting the performance of photocatalysis are light wavelength and light intensity. Fig. 11 depicts the difference in the number of photogenerated electrons generated by light with different wavelengths and intensities. Under the same light intensity, because the UV light has a shorter wavelength, higher frequency, and more energy, it is easier to excite electrons (by comparing a and b of Fig. 11). More photogenerated electrons are generated at the same time, creating more free radicals in the organic dye solution and thus increasing the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. In the case of the same wavelength, the light intensity determines the number of photons (by comparing a and c of Fig. 11). The lower the light intensity, the less the number of photons. Less number of photons are in contact with the catalyst in unit time, leading to the generation of less photogenerated electrons, less free radicals, and lower catalytic efficiency.

Fig. 12 shows the transient photocurrent response results of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs-20. When the lamp is turned on or off, the photocurrent response of the two electrodes significantly changes. After several successive switching cycles, the results are stable and reproducible. The photocurrent intensity of ZnO/CDs-20 is 2.4 times that of pure ZnO nanosheets, indicating that the separation rate of photoexcited electrons and holes of ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites is higher under visible light irradiation. One possible mechanism of this separation is that after the photogenerated electrons are excited from VB to CB of ZnO, they are transferred to CDs, thus inhibiting the direct recombination of electrons and holes. CDs may be good electron acceptors because their conjugated structure makes them an effective separation center for photoexcited charge carriers [43].

In this work, quenching experiments were also conducted. Isopropanol alcohol (IPA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were used as quenchers separately. The results are shown in Fig. 13 . It can be seen from Fig. 13 that the catalytic performance decreases obviously after adding quencher.

Based on the above results, a possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by ZnO/CDs-20 is proposed, as shown in Fig. 14 . In ZnO/CDs-20, CDs exist on some surfaces of ZnO, constituting the chemical photocatalytic system of ZnO/CDs. Under the irradiation of light, both ZnO and CDs are excited by light, resulting in e−/h+ pairs. The h+ generated in ZnO remains in the VB of ZnO. However, the e− in the CB of ZnO is transferred to the VB of the CDs and combines with the h+ in the VB of the CDs through the contact interface between CDs and ZnO. Thus, the efficient spatial separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs is achieved. Owing to the large electronegativity of oxygen molecules in water, it is easier to obtain enriched electrons on the VB of CDs and form
O 2 −
. Subsequently, the h+ stored in the VB of ZnO migrates to the surface of ZnO and reacts with
O 2 −
in water to decompose the adsorbed organic dye. Due to the reduction of electron hole recombination, more
O 2 −
is produced [2,44−47]. Based on the above discussion, the major reactions in this system are as follows:

ZnO/CDs-x (x = 10, 20, 30, 40) nanocomposites loaded with cellulose based CDs of different concentrations were prepared using hydrothermal method. Through a series of scientific experiments, the effects of CDs on the microstructure and photocatalytic activity of the samples were analyzed. The results show that composites with different morphologies can be obtained by adjusting the amount of CDs. When the concentration of CDs increases from 0 to 40, the morphology of the composite changes as follows: irregular nano flake → sphere → peony flower → rod structure. Moreover, the addition of CDs can significantly improve the photodegradation performance of MB dye. The ZnO/CDs-20 composite with 20 mL CDs exhibits the highest photocatalytic efficiency and kinetic K value, and it can effectively photodegrade four organic dyes: MB, EY, CR, and RhB. The results show that ZnO/CDs-20 photocatalyst has good stability. This can be mainly attributed to the higher absorbance, narrower band gap, and faster electron hole separation efficiency of ZnO/CDs-20 nanocomposites with a peony structure. ZnO/CDs nanocomposites have potential applications in the field of water purification.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971587).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2021.111591.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals of TiO2 photocatalysis: concepts, mechanisms, and challenges
